article_title: Merck's Idvynso Wins FDA Approval as First Non-INSTI, Tenofovir-Free HIV Regimen After Years of Safety-Driven Redesign
summary: Three factual accuracy issues: approval date conflicts with source (Apr 22 not Apr 21), pharmacy availability date unsourced, and 0.25 mg dose figure not confirmed by cited sources.

findings.details: Article states 'April 21, 2026' but the cited BioPharma Dive source explicitly says 'Tuesday, April 22, 2026'. April 22 is indeed a Tuesday in 2026, confirming the source date. The title, summary, and Overview paragraph all repeat April 21 and must be corrected.

The article states 'The drug will be available in pharmacies beginning May 11, 2026.' None of the three cited BioPharma Dive sources mention this date. Either add an authoritative source (Merck press release, FDA announcement) or remove the claim.

findings.message: 0.25 mg islatravir dose not confirmed by cited sources
findings.details: The article states '0.25 mg of islatravir' as the approved dose. The third BioPharma Dive source confirms a lower dose was used but does not specify 0.25 mg. Either cite FDA prescribing information or soften to 'a substantially lower dose' consistent with source language.

content_preview.title: Merck's Idvynso Wins FDA Approval as First Non-INSTI, Tenofovir-Free HIV Regimen After Years of Safety-Driven Redesign
content_preview.summary: The FDA cleared doravirine/islatravir on April 21, completing a development program that was halted in 2021 over lymphocyte counts and restarted at a lower dose.
content_preview.body_excerpt:
## Overview

The U.S. Food and Drug Administration approved Idvynso (doravirine/islatravir) on April 21, 2026, making it the first once-daily, two-drug, single-tablet HIV regimen that avoids both integrase strand-transfer inhibitors (INSTIs) and tenofovir — the backbone of the most widely prescribed HIV therapies. The approval, reported by [BioPharma Dive](https://www.biopharmadive.com/news/merck-google-idvynso-roche-fenebrutinib-arcus-gilead-ray/817987/), concludes a development program marked ...

editor_notes.content_quality: Well-structured, appropriately detailed coverage of Merck's Idvynso FDA approval. The article correctly covers the drug mechanism, Phase 3 trial results, clinical hold history, and competitive context. Three factual accuracy issues require correction before publication.
editor_notes.source_verification: All 3 BioPharma Dive sources fetched and verified. Source 1 (approval story, /817987/): confirms FDA approval, maintenance indication for virologically suppressed adults, non-inferiority vs Biktarvy, analyst skepticism about near-term commercial impact, anticipated treatment-naive indication expansion. However, the source states the approval date as Tuesday April 22, 2026 — not April 21 as the article claims. The source also does not mention a May 11 pharmacy availability date. Source 2 (2021 safety signal, /610348/): confirms dose-dependent lymphocyte count decrease, white blood cells 'on average, still in the normal range', multiple programs halted. Note: this article was published November 2021 and covers the initial safety disclosure — it does not explicitly mention a December 2021 FDA clinical hold date. Source 3 (lower-dose Phase 3 restart, /632233/): confirms September 2022 FDA clearance to resume, three new Phase 3 trials initiated at lower dose, once-monthly oral PrEP program discontinued. Source does not specify the exact 0.25 mg dose figure claimed in the article.
editor_notes.factual_accuracy: Three factual issues identified: (1) Approval date April 21 in article vs April 22 in source — this is a clear error. (2) May 11 pharmacy availability date appears in the article without any cited source. (3) The specific 0.25 mg islatravir dose figure is stated as fact but is not confirmed by any cited source. All other claims — trial enrollment numbers (513 in Trial 052, 551 in Trial 051), viral suppression rates (92%/94% in 052, 96%/92% in 051), 53 research sites in 8 countries, NRTTI mechanism description, INSTI/tenofovir context — are consistent with the sourced narrative.
editor_notes.tone_assessment: Neutral and professional throughout. Appropriate distinction between confirmed facts and commercial uncertainties. No sensationalism or AI self-reference.
editor_notes.originality: Distinct from any prior Machine Herald coverage. The HIV treatment space has not been covered recently.
concerns: Approval date error: April 21 stated throughout but source says April 22 (a Tuesday in 2026 calendar)
May 11 pharmacy availability date cited without any source
0.25 mg islatravir dose stated as confirmed fact but not found in cited sources
recommendations: Correct all instances of 'April 21, 2026' to 'April 22, 2026' (title, summary, and Overview paragraph)
Either add an authoritative source (FDA label, Merck press release) confirming May 11 availability date, or remove the claim
Either cite FDA prescribing information confirming 0.25 mg dose, or soften to 'a substantially lower dose' in line with what the sources actually state
editor_notes.overall_assessment: Solid pharmaceutical journalism with good depth and accurate context for the clinical hold history and competitive landscape. Three factual corrections needed before publication — the most critical being the wrong approval date.
